attributes.stem: A 28-year-old woman at 34 weeks gestation presents to the Emergency Department following a motor vehicle accident. She was a restrained front seat passenger in a high-speed collision. She has no significant medical history and takes only pregnancy supplements. On examination, she appears well. Her pulse rate is 95/min, blood pressure 110/70 mmHg, respiratory rate 18/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 36.7°C. She has bruising across her lower abdomen from the seatbelt. A Kleihauer test is strongly positive, suggesting significant fetomaternal haemorrhage.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following cardiotocograph patterns would be most suggestive of fetal anaemia in this case?
attributes.explanation:
In the context of trauma and a positive Kleihauer test indicating fetomaternal haemorrhage, the cardiotocograph pattern most suggestive of fetal anaemia would be a sinusoidal pattern. This pattern is characterised by a regular, smooth, sine wave-like oscillation of the fetal heart rate with a relatively fixed period of 2-5 cycles per minute and amplitude of 5-15 beats per minute, with very minimal beat-to-beat variability.

Sinusoidal pattern is a rare but ominous finding that is strongly associated with severe fetal anaemia. In this case, the mechanism would be acute blood loss from the fetus into the maternal circulation following trauma. This pattern requires urgent delivery and possible intrauterine transfusion depending on the clinical situation.

Early decelerations are characterised by gradual decreases in fetal heart rate that mirror the timing of contractions and represent normal head compression during labour. They are not associated with fetal anaemia.

Variable decelerations are abrupt decreases in fetal heart rate that vary in shape and timing relative to contractions. They are caused by umbilical cord compression and while they may be seen following trauma, they are not specifically associated with fetal anaemia.

Accelerations are transient increases in fetal heart rate, usually associated with fetal movement. Their presence is reassuring but their absence would not specifically suggest fetal anaemia.

Normal baseline variability refers to irregular fluctuations in fetal heart rate of 5-25 beats per minute. While loss of variability may occur in a compromised fetus, it is not specifically associated with fetal anaemia.
learningPoints: Sinusoidal fetal heart rate pattern is highly suggestive of severe fetal anaemia following fetomaternal haemorrhage.
A positive Kleihauer test indicates fetomaternal haemorrhage and should prompt urgent assessment of fetal wellbeing.
Sinusoidal pattern must persist for at least 20 minutes to be classified as true sinusoidal pattern.
references: Royal Australian and New Zealand College of Obstetricians and Gynaecologists (RANZCOG). Intrapartum Fetal Surveillance Clinical Guideline [Internet]. Melbourne: RANZCOG; 2021 [cited 2024 Nov 18]. Available from: https://ranzcog.edu.au/wp-content/uploads/2022/05/Intrapartum-Fetal-Surveillance.pdf
